target.title: Fix Azure Responses model alias routing
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `azure-openai-responses/gpt-5.5` embedded-agent/probe routes could be canonicalized through the OpenAI manifest alias and resolve with OpenAI provider/auth metadata instead of the Azure Responses provider config.
- Solution: Treat manifest model-catalog aliases with `api` or `baseUrl` overrides as transport-specific aliases that must stay on the requested provider; continue canonicalizing pure provider aliases.
- What changed: Updated embedded runner static-catalog alias canonicalization and added a regression test for Azure Responses model resolution.
- What did NOT change: Manifest catalog row planning, pure provider alias behavior such as `moonshotai -> moonshot`, or live provider API implementations.

Fixes #93781

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** `azure-openai-responses/gpt-5.5` model/probe routing keeps the Azure Responses provider, API, base URL, and auth route instead of falling back to OpenAI.
- **Real environment tested:** Darwin 25.5.0 arm64, Node v24.15.0, OpenClaw v2026.6.8, worktree SHA a0b48b8
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  ```bash
  # A local mock Responses server wrote a temporary openclaw.json with:
  # models.providers.azure-openai-responses.api=azure-openai-responses
  # models.providers.azure-openai-responses.baseUrl=<local mock>/openai/v1
  # models.providers.azure-openai-responses.models[0].id=gpt-5.5
  OPENCLAW_HOME=<temp-home> \
    OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=<temp-openclaw.json> \
    OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<temp-state> \
    pnpm openclaw models status --json --probe \
      --probe-provider azure-openai-responses \
      --probe-timeout 10000 \
      --probe-max-tokens 1

  pnpm test src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/model.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/model.static-catalog.test.ts src/model-catalog/manifest-planner.test.ts
  pnpm check:test-types
  pnpm build
  ```
- **Evidence after fix:**
  ```json
  {
    "command": "pnpm openclaw models status --json --probe --probe-provider azure-openai-responses --probe-timeout 10000 --probe-max-tokens 1",
    "exitCode": 0,
    "resolvedDefault": "azure-openai-responses/gpt-5.5",
    "defaultModel": "azure-openai-responses/gpt-5.5",
    "missingProvidersInUse": [],
    "probe": {
      "totalTargets": 1,
      "results": [
        {
          "provider": "azure-openai-responses",
          "model": "azure-openai-responses/gpt-5.5",
          "source": "models.json",
          "status": "ok"
        }
      ]
    },
    "mockRequests": [
      {
        "method": "POST",
        "url": "/openai/v1/responses?api-version=preview",
        "authorizationPresent": false,
        "apiKeyHeaderPresent": true,
        "bodyModel": "gpt-5.5",
        "stream": true,
        "maxOutputTokens": 1
      }
    ]
  }
  ```
- **Observed result after fix:** The real OpenClaw `models status --probe` path created one Azure Responses probe target, reported `azure-openai-responses/gpt-5.5`, and sent the request to the configured Azure Responses base URL with an API-key header. It did not resolve or probe the route as `openai/gpt-5.5`.
- **What was not tested:** Live Azure account credentials; the behavior proof used a local mock Responses server to verify OpenClaw routing/probe behavior without external credentials.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level: Unit test
- Target test file: `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/model.test.ts`
- Scenario locked in: Resolving `azure-openai-responses/gpt-5.5` with an explicit Azure Responses provider config returns provider `azure-openai-responses`, api `azure-openai-responses`, and the configured Azure base URL.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The regression sits on the embedded runner model resolver path that performed the alias canonicalization, while existing adjacent tests keep pure provider aliases such as `moonshotai` working.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: `canonicalizeManifestModelCatalogProviderAlias()` treated every manifest model-catalog alias as a pure provider alias. The OpenAI manifest's `azure-openai-responses` alias points at `openai` only to reuse catalog rows, but it also carries an `api` transport override, so canonicalizing it to `openai` lost the Azure Responses provider/auth/base URL identity.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Existing tests covered pure provider aliases but did not cover transport-overriding aliases that must preserve the requested provider.
